content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I started building Vidhijna with what seemed like a straightforward idea.
“Build an AI assistant that can answer legal questions, analyze documents, perform legal research, and draft legal documents.”
On paper, it sounded simple.
Take an LLM, connect it to legal documents using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), add a few prompts, and build a clean interface around it.
Reality was very different.
The more capabilities I added, the more engineering problems I created. A better research pipeline increased latency. More context increased token usage. Cloud deployment exposed memory limitations that never appeared during local development. Even something as simple as generating embeddings became a deployment challenge.
At some point, I realized I was no longer building a chatbot.
I was designing a distributed AI system where every architectural decision solved one problem while introducing another.
That realization shaped Vidhijna.
The name Vidhijna comes from Sanskrit and roughly translates to “one who understands or follows established rules.” In classical literature, it often refers to someone knowledgeable about laws, procedures, or established practices. It felt like the right name for a system built to assist with legal reasoning rather than simply generate text.
This article is not another tutorial on LangGraph, RAG, or agentic AI.
Instead, it is a walkthrough of the engineering constraints I encountered while building Vidhijna, the trade-offs behind the architecture, and the lessons I learned while turning a simple chatbot idea into a multi-agent legal AI system.
This is how the first iteration looked like
From a Simple Chatbot to a Multi-Agent System
The architecture above wasn’t my first design.
Vidhijna started as a simple RAG-powered legal chatbot — retrieve relevant legal documents, add them to the prompt, and generate an answer. That worked well for basic legal Q&A, but it quickly broke down as the project grew.
Deep legal research, document analysis, legal drafting, and web search all required different workflows. Instead of making one prompt increasingly complex, I redesigned the system around a LangGraph supervisor that routes each query to a specialized agent.
The final architecture wasn’t planned from the beginning — it evolved because every new capability introduced a new engineering constraint.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Constraint 1 — Vanilla RAG Wasn’t Enough
Vidhijna began as a simple RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant legal documents, inject them into a prompt, and generate an answer. This worked well for straightforward legal Q&A.
As the project evolved, the same pipeline had to support deep legal research, document analysis, drafting, and web search. A single prompt was now expected to route queries, choose tools, retrieve context, and generate responses — all at once.
Rather than making the prompt more complex, I redesigned the system around a LangGraph supervisor that routes requests to specialized agents, each with its own workflow and tools.
Engineering takeaway: Better AI systems are built by designing better workflows, not bigger prompts.
Constraint 2 — Better Research Meant Longer Waits
Deep legal research involved much more than a single retrieval step. It required retrieving legal documents, searching the web, summarizing multiple sources, reflecting on missing information, and combining everything into a grounded response.
The downside was increased latency, token usage, and occasional API rate limits caused by large intermediate summaries. To keep the pipeline practical, I enforced strict word limits on summaries, bounded the number of reflection loops (configurable from the UI), and used different LLMs for different tasks instead of relying on a single model. Lightweight models handled routing and summarization, while stronger models were reserved for reasoning-intensive tasks.
Engineering takeaway: Better reasoning comes from better orchestration, not unlimited computation.
Constraint 3 — Memory Changed My Document Pipeline
My original plan was straightforward: use PyPDF to extract text from text-based PDFs and a traditional OCR library for scanned documents. It worked locally, but I quickly realized it wasn’t a good fit for deployment. Running OCR locally and loading large PDFs could easily push memory usage beyond what my Cloud Run instance could comfortably handle.
Instead, I redesigned the pipeline around lazy page-by-page processing. Rather than loading the entire document into memory, each page is processed independently. For OCR, I chose Groq Vision instead of a local OCR stack. This meant making more API calls, but since Groq’s free tier covered my usage, it was a much better trade-off than increasing Cloud Run memory — which directly increases deployment cost.
The result was a lightweight backend with predictable memory usage, regardless of document size.
Engineering takeaway: Sometimes the cheapest architecture isn’t the one with fewer API calls — it’s the one that uses memory more intelligently.
Constraint 4 — The Cloud Forced Different Engineering Decisions
During development, I used the Hugging Face Inference API to generate embeddings, and everything worked perfectly on my local machine. However, after deploying to Google Cloud Run, embedding requests started failing with authentication issues because the application was running inside a cloud container.
Instead of relying on an external embedding service, I switched to FastEmbed with local ONNX embedding models. The embedding model is bundled directly into the Docker image, eliminating API dependencies, token limits, and first-request model downloads.
Another reason for this change was deployment size. Libraries such as sentence-transformers and the full Hugging Face stack would have significantly increased the Docker image size. FastEmbed is lightweight, the ONNX models are relatively small, and the final Docker image remained under 500 MB, allowing it to comfortably fit within the Google Cloud free-tier storage limits while keeping startup time predictable.
This made the deployment completely self-contained — the container always starts with the embedding model already available, regardless of network availability or external service limits.
Engineering takeaway: Cloud-native AI systems should minimize external dependencies and optimize for predictable deployment, not just local development.
Constraint 5 — Long-Running AI Needs Continuous Feedback
Deep research and document analysis can take tens of seconds. Returning a single HTTP response made the application feel frozen even when the agents were actively working.
I switched to Server-Sent Events (SSE) with FastAPI to stream node execution, research progress, citations, and the final response. Instead of waiting silently, users can see how the system is reasoning in real time.
Engineering takeaway: Good user experience isn’t just about faster AI — it’s about visible progress.
Constraint 6— Deployment Needed to Be Repeatable
Another challenge wasn’t related to AI at all — it was deployment.
Since I develop on macOS, the Docker images built locally weren’t always compatible with the Linux environment used by Google Cloud Run. Initially, I considered building and pushing images manually (or using Jenkins), but maintaining that workflow quickly became inconvenient.
Instead, I moved the entire build pipeline to GitHub Actions. Every push to the repository now automatically builds a Linux-compatible Docker image, pushes it to Google Artifact Registry, and deploys the latest version to Cloud Run.
Besides solving the platform compatibility issue, it also gave me a much cleaner CI/CD pipeline. Deployments became consistent, repeatable, and required nothing more than pushing new code.
Engineering takeaway: Automating deployment isn’t just about convenience — it removes environment-specific problems and makes the system easier to maintain.
What Building Vidhijna Taught Me
When I started building Vidhijna, I thought the difficult part would be choosing the right LLM or writing better prompts.
It wasn’t.
The real challenge was designing a system that could balance retrieval quality, latency, memory, deployment, and user experience without becoming overly complex.
Looking back, every major improvement came from solving an engineering constraint rather than adding another model or writing a better prompt.
The project completely changed how I think about building AI applications.
Today, I spend far more time thinking about workflows, architecture, deployment, and observability than prompt engineering.
Agentic AI is ultimately a systems engineering problem, not just an LLM problem.
Final Thoughts
Vidhijna is still evolving. There are many things I want to improve — better evaluation, caching, smarter retrieval strategies, and more efficient research workflows.
But if this project taught me one thing, it’s this:
The quality of an AI application is influenced by both the model and the architecture — but as systems become more complex, architecture often becomes the deciding factor.
If you’re building an agentic application, spend less time searching for the perfect prompt and more time designing the right workflow. Models will continue to improve, but good system design will always remain valuable.
